patterns.name: Condensation Mix-Up
patterns.what: Enxin sometimes confuses evaporation with condensation, or forgets to mention that warm water vapour must lose heat to a cooler surface to form droplets.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: Enxin correctly identifies that the water will evaporate, but forgets to explain that the warm water vapour must touch the cooler metal tray and lose heat to condense into droplets.
Enxin mentions that the ice gains heat, but misses the crucial step where warmer water vapour from the surrounding air touches the cooler beaker and condenses.
Enxin thinks the cooler surface causes evaporation to happen faster, mixing it up with the process of condensation.

patterns.strategic_advice:
When you see questions about **water droplets**, **mist**, or a **white cloud** forming, treat it as a signal to write out the full 'condensation story':
- Start with **'warmer water vapour'** from the surroundings or the hot liquid.
- Mention that it **'touches a cooler surface'**.
- End with **'loses heat and condenses'** into water droplets.

patterns.name: Air Volume Mix-Up
patterns.what: Enxin sometimes calculates the final volume of air by adding or subtracting the amounts pumped, forgetting that air always expands or compresses to fill the available space.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: Enxin adds the 200 cm³ of pumped air to the 500 cm³ tank, forgetting that air simply compresses to fit the container's original volume.
Enxin correctly adds the volume of the pumped water, but also adds the volume of the pumped air, missing that the air will just squeeze into whatever space is left.
Enxin subtracts the drawn-out air from the total volume, forgetting that the remaining air will just expand to fill the empty space.

patterns.strategic_advice:
When you see a **pump** attached to a **sealed container**, remember this golden rule:
- **Water has a fixed volume**, so it takes up space and cannot be squished.
- **Air has no fixed volume**, so it will always stretch or compress to fill whatever empty space is left in the container. Never just add or subtract the air volume!

patterns.name: Digestion Journey Mix-Up
patterns.what: Enxin occasionally mixes up what happens at each stage of digestion, such as forgetting that digestion begins in the mouth or that only undigested food reaches the large intestine.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: Enxin selects an option stating that digestion starts in the stomach, forgetting that the process actually begins in the mouth with saliva.
Enxin thinks that digested food is passed to the large intestine, when in fact only undigested food and water end up there.
Enxin chooses a graph showing incorrect amounts of digested food, mixing up how much digestion happens in the mouth, stomach, and small intestine.

patterns.strategic_advice:
When you see questions about the **digestive system**, trace the food's journey step-by-step:
- **Mouth**: Digestion starts here (saliva).
- **Small Intestine**: Digestion ends here, and the good stuff (digested food) enters the blood.
- **Large Intestine**: Only the leftovers (**undigested food** and water) go here. No digestion happens here!

patterns.name: Plant Tubes Mix-Up
patterns.what: Enxin tends to confuse the roles of food-carrying and water-carrying tubes, or forgets that water must be transported to every single part of the plant to keep it alive.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: Enxin struggles to predict what happens to the leaves when the outer rings of the stem are removed, confusing the roles of the food-carrying and water-carrying tubes.
Enxin mixes up the inner and outer layers of the stem when asked to identify what remains after the food-carrying tubes are removed.
Enxin thinks water-carrying tubes are only found in the roots and stems, forgetting that they must reach the leaves and flowers too.

patterns.strategic_advice:
When you see a question about a plant's **stem** being cut or having its **outer rings removed**, use this quick check:
- **Water-carrying tubes** are on the inside and must go to **every single part** of the plant (roots, stems, leaves, flowers).
- **Food-carrying tubes** are on the outside (the bark) and carry food from the leaves to the rest of the plant.
